content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
PO BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
916 322 5660        322 3901         322 5441         322 6444

October 19, 1977

John A. Shahabian
Senate Select Committee
on Small Business Enterprises
1116 Ninth Street, Suite 71
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Shahabian:

Thank you for your letter of September 27, 1977. You
indicated in your letter and in telephone conversations with me
that the Senate Select Committee on Small Business Enterprises
maintains a voluntary advisory board of private individuals. The
board transmits ideas and suggestions for legislative changes af-
fecting small business to the Committee staff and makes small
business owners aware of current legislative activity. You indi-
cate that the Society of Industrial Realtors wishes to provide
the advisory board with an office and secretarial assistance in
Los Angeles. You ask whether this activity will cause any report-
ing obligation for Senator David Roberti, who is chairman of the
Committee. You indicate further that the advisory board is com-
pletely nonpartisan and does not involve itself in any political
campaigns.

Neither the activities of the board nor the office space
and secretarial services funded by the Society of Industrial
Realtors would be reportable by Senator Roberti as a campaign con-
tribution or a gift. If the advisory board became involved in an
election campaign, it might then incur reporting obligations of
its own, and if Senator Roberti as a candidate benefited from
such activity, Senator Roberti may have reporting obligations as
well. So long as the advisory board does not involve itself in
political campaigns, however, no reporting of this type would be
required.

It is possible that the activities of the advisory board
may give rise to reporting requirements under the lobbyist provi-
sions of the Act. You have indicated that the Society of Industrial

John A. Shahabian
P.2
October 19, 1977


Realtors intends to make available office space and secretarial
assistance to the advisory board, as opposed to providing funds
to the advisory board to use for that purpose. Under these circumstances, the Society of Industrial Realtors will be making payments to "influence legislative or administrative action" within
the meaning of Government Code Section 82045(e) and would be required to file a report disclosing its activity for any month in
which it spends $250 or more on such activity. See Government
Code Sections 86108(b) and 86109. The advisory board itself,
however, will not have any reporting obligations.

It is also possible that some of the members of the
advisory board may become lobbyists by virtue of their involvement on the advisory board. The standards for determining whether
a person spends enough time engaged in legislative advocacy to
become a lobbyist within the meaning of the Act, with the attendant reporting obligations, is contained in Commission regulation
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(e)(2)(A), a copy of which is enclosed for your review. You should note that even if the time thresholds set forth in that regulation are met, a person must be compensated for his time in order to become a lobbyist.

Finally, even if the persons who serve on the advisory
board are not lobbyists, it is possible that their employers may
incur a filing obligation by spending $250 or more in a month to
influence legislative or administrative action. This would occur
only if the employee serving on the board spends more than 10 percent of his compensated time during any one month engaged in board
activity. If the 10 percent threshold is met, the portion of the
employee's compensation attributable to the advisory board activities, including any expenses incurs, should be taken into account to determine if the $250 threshold is met. I have enclosed
Commission regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18621 which sets
out the standards for determining whether an employer incurs a
filing obligation because of the activities of its employees related to influencing legislative or administrative action.

I hope this answers your questions. If I can be of any
further assistance please contact me.

Sincerely,

Michael S. Baker
Chief
Legal Division

MJB:jo

Enclosures

JOHN A. SHAHABIAN
CONSULTANT

CALIFORNIA LEGISLATURE

SENATE SELECT COMMITTEE
ON
SMALL BUSINESS ENTERPRISES

DAVID A. ROBERTI
CHAIRMAN

COMMITTEE ADDRESS
1116 9TH STREET, 271
9TH AND L BUILDING
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
916/322-3960

September 27, 1977

Mr. Mike Baker, Staff Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street, Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Baker:

This letter confirms our telephone conversation this morning and
requests your reply in writing.

This committee maintains a voluntary advisory board of private
individuals interested in small business assistance. Our Committee
plays a large part in the administration of the advisory board, al-
though the day to day operation is guided by two chairmen appointed
by Senator Roberti, one from Northern California, one from Southern
California.

One of the functions of the board is to transmit ideas and sugges-
tions for legislative others from individual small businesses to
the committee staff. Another function is to make small business
owners aware of current legislative proposals and to encourage
their involvement in the legislative process of approval of these
proposals.

In order to better fulfill these functions the advisory board lead-
ership proposes to maintain an office and secretarial assistance
in Los Angeles which would be donated by the Society of Industrial
Realtors. We have asked them to not accept such assistance until
we have checked with your office.

Of course, the advisory board is completely nonpartisan and may not
involve itself in any political campaigns.

We would like to know if assistance given to the advisory board
will be construed as reportable by Senator Roberti as a campaign
contribution or gift. What conditions or circumstances would make

Mr. Mike Baker, FPPC                    -2-                    September 27, 1977


such assistance reportable? What sections govern such assistance
to advisory boards of legislative committees?

Your timely reply will facilitate the operation of the advisory
board to this committee.

Sincerely,

[signature]
JOHN A. GRAHABIAN

JAS:bh

sections.question: We have asked them to not accept such assistance until we have checked with your office. Of course, the advisory board is completely nonpartisan and may not involve itself in any political campaigns. We would like to know if assistance given to the advisory board will be construed as reportable by Senator Roberti as a campaign contribution or gift. What conditions or circumstances would make such assistance reportable? What sections govern such assistance to advisory boards of legislative Committees?
sections.conclusion: Neither the activities of the board nor the office space and secretarial services funded by the Society of Industrial Realtors would be reportable by Senator Roberti as a campaign contribution or gift, so long as the advisory board does not involve itself in political campaigns. However, the Society of Industrial Realtors will be required to file a report disclosing its activity under the lobbyist provisions of the Act if it spends $250 or more in a month on such activity. Depending on the time spent and compensation involved, members of the advisory board and their employers may also incur filing obligations under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does providing office space and secretarial assistance to a legislative committee's nonpartisan advisory board trigger reporting obligations for the Senator who chairs the committee or the organization providing the assistance?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that assistance to the nonpartisan advisory board is not reportable as a campaign contribution if no political campaigning is involved, but the Society of Industrial Realtors must report under the lobbyist provisions if monthly spending reaches $250 or more, and board members or their employers may have additional reporting obligations depending on time and compensation thresholds.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (dated September 27, 1977) and the FPPC response letter (dated October 19, 1977) from Chief Legal Counsel. Some OCR scanning artifacts present but do not significantly impact content extraction. Clear Q/A structure with enclosed regulatory references.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: We have asked them to not accept such assistance until we have checked with your office. Of course, the advisory board is completely nonpartisan and may not involve itself in any political campaigns. We would like to know if assistance given to the advisory board will be construed as reportable by Senator Roberti as a campaign contribution or gift. What conditions or circumstances would make such assistance reportable? What sections govern such assistance to advisory boards of legislative Committees?

CONCLUSION: Neither the activities of the board nor the office space and secretarial services funded by the Society of Industrial Realtors would be reportable by Senator Roberti as a campaign contribution or gift, so long as the advisory board does not involve itself in political campaigns. However, the Society of Industrial Realtors will be required to file a report disclosing its activity under the lobbyist provisions of the Act if it spends $250 or more in a month on such activity. Depending on the time spent and compensation involved, members of the advisory board and their employers may also incur filing obligations under the Act.

embedding.summary: An FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry about whether office space and secretarial assistance from the Society of Industrial Realtors to a Senate Select Committee's voluntary advisory board on small business creates reporting obligations for Senator David Roberti or the donor organization.
